original_hypothesis.title: ILOManager Constructor Uses tx.origin for Ownership Creating Deployment Security Gap
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ILOManager constructor calls `transferOwnership(tx.origin)`. When deployed via a factory, Create2 deployer, multisig, or deployment pipeline, tx.origin is the EOA that initiated the outer transaction, not necessarily the intended owner. Between deployment and initialize() (which transfers ownership to initialOwner), the tx.origin address has full owner privileges and can call setPlatformFee, setPerformanceFee, setFeeTaker, setILOPoolImplementation, etc. The deployment script (Deploy.s.sol) deploys and initializes in the same broadcast, but if initialize() fails or is called separately, the window exists.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: tx.origin-based ownership is a known anti-pattern. The deployment script partially mitigates by initializing in the same transaction, but separate deployment/initialization (Init.s.sol exists as separate script) creates a real window.
deployment_assumptions: ILOManager deployment and initialization happen in separate transactions
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Init.s.sol exists as a separate initialization script, indicating separate deployment/init is a supported workflow

proof_of_signal.pattern: constructor () { transferOwnership(tx.origin); } - tx.origin used instead of msg.sender

preconditions: ILOManager deployed via factory/multisig where tx.origin != intended owner
Gap between deployment and initialize() call
tx.origin address is aware of ownership or malicious

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: tx.origin ownership in constructor + separate Init.s.sol deployment script

operational_questions: Is ILOManager always deployed and initialized atomically?
Can the tx.origin owner call setILOPoolImplementation before initialize()?
Does the Initializable modifier protect against owner abuse before initialization?
validation_requirements: Check if owner functions work before initialize() (afterInitialize guard on some functions)
Test tx.origin ownership with factory deployment

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor for ILOManager deployment without immediate initialization
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: tx.origin (if compromised) calls setILOPoolImplementation with malicious implementation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All future ILO pools cloned from malicious implementation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: initialize() called later, transfers ownership away, but damage is done

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Temporary full admin access to ILOManager
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Malicious pool implementation affecting all future pools
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner can fix after initialization, but early pools may be compromised

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Owner function calls between deployment and initialization
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Multiple ownership transfers in rapid succession

validation_result.impact_summary: ILOManager constructor sets owner to tx.origin, creating a window between deployment and initialization where an unintended EOA has owner privileges. DEFAULT_DEADLINE_OFFSET changes persist through initialization.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The ILOManager constructor at line 29-31 calls `transferOwnership(tx.origin)`. This is a documented anti-pattern (SWC-115). The code comment acknowledges deployment via a deployer contract, but tx.origin introduces risks:

1. When deployed via Create2 deployer (0x4e59b44847b379578588920cA78FbF26c0B4956C as shown in Common.s.sol), msg.sender is the deployer factory but tx.origin is the initiating EOA.

2. The codebase provides both atomic (Deploy.s.sol AllContractScript) and separate (ILOManagerScript + Init.s.sol) deployment workflows. The separate workflow creates an explicit window.

3. During the window between deployment and initialize(), the tx.origin owner can call onlyOwner functions. While most state (PLATFORM_FEE, PERFORMANCE_FEE, FEE_TAKER, ILO_POOL_IMPLEMENTATION) gets overwritten by initialize(), DEFAULT_DEADLINE_OFFSET is NOT set by initialize() and persists.

4. The initialize() function has NO onlyOwner guard - only whenNotInitialized - so the tx.origin owner cannot prevent initialization, but CAN make persistent state changes before it.

5. Even in the AllContractScript, deployment and initialization are separate on-chain transactions (within the same broadcast block), creating a small window.

The defect is in the use of tx.origin itself - it's the wrong mechanism for determining the intended owner, regardless of exploitation feasibility.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: ILOManager.sol lines 29-31 confirm `transferOwnership(tx.origin)` in the constructor. The initialize() function at lines 33-46 sets most state variables but notably omits DEFAULT_DEADLINE_OFFSET. Deploy.s.sol lines 36-39 show ILOManagerScript deploys without initializing. Init.s.sol provides a separate initialization script confirming the split workflow is supported.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender or a constructor parameter. When deployed via Create2 deployer proxy, multisig, or factory, tx.origin is the initiating EOA which may not be the intended owner. Creates a privilege window before initialize() is called.
Does NOT reset DEFAULT_DEADLINE_OFFSET, so any change made by the tx.origin owner before initialization persists. Also has no access control (no onlyOwner), only whenNotInitialized guard.
This onlyOwner function can be called by tx.origin owner before initialize(), and the value persists since initialize() does not set DEFAULT_DEADLINE_OFFSET.

calling_sequence: 1. EOA calls Create2 deployer factory (0x4e59b44847b379578588920cA78FbF26c0B4956C) to deploy ILOManager
2. Constructor executes: Ownable() sets _owner = msg.sender (factory), then transferOwnership(tx.origin) sets owner to initiating EOA
3. Window opens: tx.origin EOA is now owner, _initialized = false
4. tx.origin can call setDefaultDeadlineOffset(0) setting DEFAULT_DEADLINE_OFFSET to 0
5. initialize() is called later (separate tx), overwrites most state but NOT DEFAULT_DEADLINE_OFFSET
6. DEFAULT_DEADLINE_OFFSET = 0 means refundDeadline = launchTime + 0 = launchTime for future projects
7. This means refund is immediately available at launch time, creating race conditions
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The constructor at ILOManager.sol:29-31 sets owner to tx.origin. Between deployment and initialization, the tx.origin owner can call any onlyOwner function. setDefaultDeadlineOffset (line 131) modifies DEFAULT_DEADLINE_OFFSET which is not reset by initialize() (lines 33-46). The ILOManagerScript in Deploy.s.sol:36-44 only deploys without initializing, and Init.s.sol exists as a separate initialization workflow.

deployment_assumptions_checked: ILOManager deployment and initialization happen in separate transactions
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deploy.s.sol contains ILOManagerScript (lines 36-44) that deploys ILOManager WITHOUT calling initialize(). Init.s.sol (ILOManagerInitializeScript) is a separate script that only calls initialize(). This confirms separate deployment/initialization is an explicitly supported workflow. Even AllContractScript performs deployment and initialization as separate on-chain transactions within a single broadcast block.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is valid: (1) ILOManager is deployed via ILOManagerScript or through any deployer contract. (2) tx.origin becomes owner. (3) Before initialize() is called (could be in same block or later), tx.origin calls setDefaultDeadlineOffset(0). (4) initialize() is called, transferring ownership to initialOwner, but DEFAULT_DEADLINE_OFFSET remains 0. (5) All future projects created via initProject will have refundDeadline = launchTime + 0 = launchTime, meaning refund is available immediately at launch time, creating a race condition between launch() and claimRefund(). This requires tx.origin to be aware and act, but the code defect enabling it is real.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM impact. The tx.origin anti-pattern creates a privilege escalation window. The most persistent damage is modification of DEFAULT_DEADLINE_OFFSET (not reset by initialize()). Setting it to 0 would mean all future projects have refundDeadline = launchTime, enabling immediate refund at launch time. Other owner-settable values (fees, fee taker, implementation) are overwritten by initialize(). The initProject function requires afterInitialize guard, preventing project/pool creation before initialization.
recommended_mitigations: Replace tx.origin with msg.sender in constructor, or better yet, accept the initial owner as a constructor parameter
Add DEFAULT_DEADLINE_OFFSET to the initialize() function parameters so it is set during initialization
Add onlyOwner modifier to initialize() function to prevent front-running
Consider using OpenZeppelin's Initializable pattern that disables the constructor entirely for clone patterns
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy ILOManager via Create2 deployer factory using Foundry script (ILOManagerScript). 2. From the tx.origin EOA, call setDefaultDeadlineOffset(0) before initialization. 3. Call initialize() with proper parameters from any address. 4. Verify DEFAULT_DEADLINE_OFFSET is 0 instead of the expected 7 days. 5. Create a project via initProject and verify refundDeadline equals launchTime.
uncertainties: In practice, deployment and initialization in AllContractScript happen in rapid succession, making the window very small for the atomic deployment path
The tx.origin EOA must be aware of and willing to exploit the window - accidental exploitation is unlikely
Foundry broadcast semantics: whether new Contract{salt:} goes through the external deployer factory or uses CREATE2 directly depends on Foundry version and configuration
additional_testing_needed: Verify that DEFAULT_DEADLINE_OFFSET persists through initialize() call
Test the separate ILOManagerScript + Init.s.sol deployment path and confirm the window exists
Verify tx.origin behavior when deploying through the Create2 deployer proxy
